findings.description: The primary>secondary>tertiary application hierarchy (dominant blues/golds, tertiary as accents) is a distinct governing rule but has no corresponding candidate rule; it is currently unrepresented in the cluster.

payload.summary: Apply palette in primary>secondary>tertiary ratio; blues/golds dominate, tertiary is accent/surface only

findings.description: Headline/heading color specification (Brand Blue, White on dark) is a discrete color_application rule missing from the candidate set.

payload.summary: Headlines: Brand Blue (or White on dark/gradient backgrounds)

findings.description: Body text color specification (Graphite on light, White on dark) is a discrete color_application rule missing from the candidate set, and relates to line-height cross-reference (§1.2) not captured anywhere.

payload.summary: Body text: Graphite #212121 on light backgrounds; White on dark backgrounds

findings.description: Chart color rule with explicit hard-constraint language ('Sunshine/Gold strictly reserved for emphasizing key figures') is entirely absent from the candidate rules, losing a strong prohibition/reservation signal.

payload.summary: Charts: Brand Blue + Sky Blue dominant; Sunshine/Gold strictly reserved for key figures; gray for placebo

findings.description: The Coral restriction ('never for primary CTAs, headlines, or data') is a hard prohibition rule with a [GENERAL] governance tag for email sections, but no corresponding rule exists in the candidate set.

payload.summary: Coral is a sparing tertiary accent only; never for primary CTAs, headlines, or data; avoid in email sections unless brief calls for it

findings.description: Source unit carries an explicit [GENERAL] governance tag scoping the link color rule, but the candidate rule's payload does not appear to record this governance/scope facet.

Source unit merely states tint percentages for callout surfaces with no imperative/directive language ('must', 'never', etc.), yet the rule is classified as hard_constraint/must. Hardness appears overstated versus a strong_default or descriptive guidance.

Source describes link color/underline treatment declaratively without 'must' or similar imperative; polarity 'must' with hardness strong_default may overstate the directive strength versus a recommended default.
